skill.name: linear-projects-write
skill.description: Create and update Linear projects from CLI commands using a configured Linear API key.
skill.summary: Manage Linear project creation and updates through documented Bun CLI commands.
skill.author: Bind

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command, credential, filesystem, and network indicators, but most command and cryptography alerts are markdown syntax false positives. The remaining real risk is legitimate but meaningful: this community skill documents Bun CLI write operations for Linear and requires LINEAR_API_KEY from the environment or a .env file.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Linear Write Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill requires LINEAR_API_KEY and documents create and update commands for Linear projects. This is expected for the stated purpose, but misuse or overbroad tokens could modify production project data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly document a Linear API key and CLI commands that create or update projects. No malicious exfiltration is present in the scanned files, but the intended write capability creates real operational risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many markdown backticks and shell examples as Ruby or shell execution. In the scanned files these are documentation examples, not executable code paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside markdown code fences that show how to invoke the CLI. No script body, dynamic shell construction, or Ruby backtick expression exists in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not match any cryptographic implementation in the scanned files. The visible content is metadata and CLI documentation only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hash, cipher, random number, or signature code at the reported lines. The scanner appears to have matched ordinary documentation text.

low_findings.title: Documented .env Access for API Key Loading
low_findings.description: The skill tells users to place LINEAR_API_KEY in a .env file or environment variable. This is common configuration, but users should avoid committing the .env file or sharing broad personal tokens.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential source is plainly documented and aligns with Linear API usage. The scanned files do not show credential exfiltration, so the concern is secret handling rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key from Environment or .env
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on LINEAR_API_KEY from the environment or a git-root .env file. This requires careful token storage and least-privilege Linear permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both the manifest and documentation name LINEAR_API_KEY as required setup. This is a real credential dependency even though it is consistent with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Commands with Write Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented Bun commands create and update Linear projects. Running them can change project names, leads, statuses, dates, and priorities.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command names and options explicitly describe create and update actions. The scanned files do not include implementation code, so the assessment is based on documented behavior.

content.user_title: Manage Linear Projects from the CLI
content.value_statement: Teams often need quick Linear project changes without opening the web app. This skill documents commands for creating and updating Linear projects with a configured API key.
actual_capabilities: Create Linear projects with a name and one or more teams.
Set project description, lead, status, dates, and priority during creation.
Update existing projects by UUID or partial project name.
Remove or change project leads through the update command.
Request machine-readable command output with the JSON flag.
limitations: Requires Bun and a valid LINEAR_API_KEY before commands can run.
The scanned package contains documentation and manifest files, not implementation source files.
Partial project name matching can select the wrong project if names are similar.
Write operations can affect live Linear workspace data.

use_cases.title: Create launch projects quickly
use_cases.description: Create a Linear project with teams, lead, target date, and priority from one command.

use_cases.title: Keep project status current
use_cases.description: Update status, dates, or owners during planning and delivery reviews.

use_cases.title: Automate project setup
use_cases.description: Use documented CLI options in repeatable project creation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic project
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Linear project named Customer Portal for the Engineering team. Use normal priority and show me the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create with planning fields
prompt_templates.scenario: Project setup with dates and owner
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a command to create a Linear project named Q3 Reliability for Engineering and Product. Set the lead to Alex Chen, status planned, and target date 2026-09-30.

prompt_templates.title: Update an existing project
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the Linear project named Checkout Migration to started, set priority high, and move the target date to 2026-08-15. Confirm the matched project first.

prompt_templates.title: Review a batch of changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft commands to update three Linear projects with new leads and target dates. Do not run them until I approve each matched project name.

output_examples.input: Create a Linear project for a security audit with Engineering as the team and a March target date.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a Bun create-project command with the project name, team, start date, target date, and priority options.

output_examples.input: Mark the Mount Vernon project completed.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the update-project command, asks for confirmation if multiple projects may match, and then applies the completed status.

output_examples.input: Change the project lead to none and return machine-readable output.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses the lead removal option and includes the JSON output flag for downstream tooling.

best_practices: Use a Linear API key with the minimum permissions needed for project updates.
Confirm project matches before using partial names in update commands.
Review generated commands before running them against production workspaces.
anti_patterns: Do not commit LINEAR_API_KEY or .env files to source control.
Do not use broad personal tokens for shared automation workflows.
Do not run partial-name updates when multiple Linear projects may match.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It documents CLI commands for creating and updating Linear projects using a configured API key.

faq.question: Does it need a Linear API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation requires LINEAR_API_KEY in the environment or a git-root .env file.

faq.question: Can it update existing projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It can update projects by UUID or by a partial project name match.

faq.question: Can it change project leads?
faq.answer: Yes. It can set a project lead by name or remove the lead with the none value.

faq.question: Does it support structured output?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented commands include a JSON output flag for machine-readable results.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: The commands perform write operations in Linear, so users should confirm targets and protect their API key.
